nativeRange.habitat: Blackwater rivers and flooded forest tributaries; tannin-stained, slow-moving water over leaf litter and sandy substrate with minimal light penetration

decorPreferences: dark substrate (fine sand or dark gravel)
driftwood and dried leaf litter for tannins
dense planting around periphery (Amazon swords, Java fern, Vallisneria)

diet.notes: Accepts quality flake and micro-granule foods as a staple. Regular offerings of small live and frozen foods — baby brine shrimp, daphnia, micro-worms, and cyclops — are important for vibrant red coloration and long-term health. Color-enhancing foods containing krill or astaxanthin are beneficial. Small mouth size requires appropriately sized particles.

avoidWith: boisterous or fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding
baby shrimp and fish fry (predation risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase thumbnail for Petitella bleheri (syn. Hemigrammus bleheri); verify full-res availability before upload.

summary: The Firehead Tetra is the brightest and most widely traded member of the rummy-nose complex, distinguished by intense red coloration extending beyond the snout onto the operculum (gill cover). A schooling blackwater tetra requiring stable, soft, acidic water and groups of 10 or more for characteristic tight-formation schooling.
careNotes:
The Firehead Tetra (Hemigrammus bleheri) is the species most commonly sold as “rummy-nose tetra” in the modern aquarium trade — it accounts for the vast majority of commercial imports. It belongs to a three-species “rummy-nose complex” that requires care when identifying specimens: (1) Hemigrammus rhodostomus (“Common Rummy-Nose,” fw-fish-125) — red confined to the snout, not extending past the eye; (2) Hemigrammus bleheri (“Firehead Tetra,” THIS entry, fw-fish-183) — the BRIGHTEST of the three, with red extending well onto the operculum (gill cover) and into the humeral region; (3) Petitella georgiae (“False Rummy-Nose,” fw-fish-181) — the least red, with a distinctive black body stripe extending forward from the caudal peduncle. In H. bleheri the anal fin is hyaline (clear), helping distinguish it from P. georgiae.

This species is a sensitive water-quality indicator — aquarists commonly use the caudal fin pattern (bold black-and-white checkerboard) and the red head coloration as a “barometer”: fading of the red and/or pallor of the caudal bars is a reliable early warning of elevated nitrates, ammonia, or temperature stress. Maintain nitrates below 20 ppm and perform regular 25–30% water changes weekly.

Keep in groups of 10 or more; smaller groups exhibit stress, reduced color, and corner-hugging behavior. The characteristic tight schooling formation only emerges in adequate numbers. A tank of at least 80 L with a 90 cm footprint is recommended; longer tanks are preferable to allow schooling runs. Soft, acidic, tannin-stained water (pH 5.5–7.0, gH 2–15) replicates the blackwater Rio Negro and Rio Meta biotopes. Peat filtration or commercially prepared blackwater extract (Indian almond leaves, alder cones) is strongly advised; colors fade noticeably in alkaline or hard water. Temperature should be stable at 23–28°C; sudden fluctuations cause immediate color loss and immune suppression.

The species was described by Géry and Mahnert in 1986 and named in honor of Heiko Bleher, the German aquarist and explorer who first collected and shipped specimens for scientific identification. A 2020 molecular phylogenetic study reclassified it to the genus Petitella (as Petitella bleheri), placing it as a sister species to P. georgiae; many hobby and trade sources continue to use the original Hemigrammus bleheri name.
breedingNotes: Moderately challenging egg scatterer. A dedicated breeding tank of approximately 40–60 L is recommended, with very soft water (gH 1–5), pH 5.5–6.5, and temperature raised slightly to 26–28°C. Use fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort) or synthetic spawning mops and a mesh grid over the bottom to protect eggs from the egg-eating parents. Dim lighting is important. Condition the breeding pair or small group with live and frozen foods for 1–2 weeks prior to spawning. Females scatter small, adhesive eggs among plants; remove adults promptly after spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–36 hours. Fry are light-sensitive initially; keep the tank dark. Feed with infusoria or commercial fry foods for the first week, progressing to microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii as they grow. Breeding in captivity is achievable but relatively uncommon given wild-caught fish are abundantly available through the ornamental trade.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hemigrammus bleheri
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided water parameters (23–26°C, pH 5.5–7.0, hardness 2–15°H), minimum tank length (90 cm), group size (10+), diet, breeding protocol, and key distinguishing features of H. bleheri vs rhodostomus and P. georgiae (red extending past gill covers, hyaline anal fin).

additional.name: FishBase — Petitella bleheri (syn. Hemigrammus bleheri)
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (3.6 cm SL), temperature (23–26°C), pH (5.0–6.0), hardness (5–12 dH), native range (Rio Negro and Rio Meta basins; Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela), trophic level 3.1 (omnivore), taxonomy (Characiformes). Notes 2020 reclassification to Petitella bleheri under Acestrorhamphidae; per task brief family listed as Characidae (traditional hobbyist convention).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rummy-Nose Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide. Confirmed temperature 23–29°C, adult size ~5 cm, minimum tank 20 gal (~75 L), schooling group 8–12, lifespan up to 5+ years, diet (baby brine shrimp, nano pellets, daphnia, color-enhancing foods), compatibility with peaceful community fish. Identified three-species complex (H. rhodostomus, H. bleheri, P. georgiae). Used 80 L / 90 cm minimum per SeriouslyFish as more species-specific guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hemigrammus bleheri
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (middle Rio Negro and upper/middle Rio Orinoco basins; Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela), etymology (named for Heiko Bleher), 2020 molecular reclassification to Petitella bleheri, distinguishing features (red to humeral region, hyaline anal fin, caudal bar does not extend far forward), and ornamental trade export figures (7.1 million specimens from Amazonas state 2006–2015).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Rummy-Nose Tetra (lifespan supplementary)
additional.notes: Aquarium Breeder detailed guide confirmed lifespan range 5–8 years in well-maintained aquaria; corroborated diet, schooling behavior, and blackwater biotope preferences.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; requires dedicated soft-water breeding tank (pH 5.5–6.5, gH 1–5, 26–28°C). Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry begin on infusoria then microworms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. Remove adults immediately after spawning to prevent egg predation. Rarely bred in captivity given abundant wild-caught availability.
